name: The Haunted Mumbai Apartment
description: Residents of a residential flat in Mumbai reported repeated sightings of shadowed figures, unexplained sounds occurring at consistent intervals, and an overwhelming physical sensation of being watched. Three separate families vacated the apartment over eighteen months. IPS investigators spent four nights on-site before identifying the source: infrasound generated by industrial ventilation machinery two buildings away, transmitted through shared structural beams. Low-frequency vibrations at 18-19Hz — the precise range known to cause unease, visual disturbances, and hallucination-adjacent perceptions — were detected at consistent readings throughout the flat. Once the building management redirected the ventilation route, all reports ceased. No paranormal conclusion drawn. Case remains a reference point for infrasound misidentification. Case classification: RESOLVED — Environmental Cause Confirmed.

name: Spirit Possession — Uttar Pradesh
description: A young individual in rural Uttar Pradesh began displaying rapid behavioral changes, speaking in unfamiliar voices, and experiencing physical distress that the community unanimously attributed to spirit possession. Several local religious figures had already conducted extended exorcism rituals lasting over three weeks before the family contacted IPS. Investigators conducted structured interviews with the individual, the family, and community witnesses. Psychological evaluation suggested dissociative symptoms triggered by acute stress from a domestic situation that had not been previously disclosed. IPS investigators documented their findings formally and recommended immediate psychological and medical care. The family initially refused, citing community pressure. IPS filed documentation with a local mental health outreach organization. Follow-up confirmed the individual received treatment and recovered. Case flagged: Category P-02. Community reinforcement of paranormal belief identified as primary obstacle to care.

name: Carbon Monoxide Hallucination — Delhi NCR
source: Indian Paranormal Society — Critical Case File
description: Over six weeks, multiple residents across two floors of a Delhi NCR residential building reported apparitions appearing in peripheral vision, persistent headaches, irrational fear, paranoia, and in two cases, full visual hallucinations of figures that could not be physically located. Three residents had already consulted religious practitioners and one had abandoned the apartment entirely before IPS was contacted. Investigators followed protocol — environmental testing before any psychological or paranormal evaluation. CO detectors placed in shared utility corridors and stairwells confirmed dangerous carbon monoxide accumulation from a faulty gas heating appliance in a ground-floor utility room. The leak was slow, below immediately lethal levels, but sufficient to produce documented neurological effects over extended exposure. Appliance repaired, building ventilated. All symptoms across all affected residents ceased within 72 hours. No residual reports. Case is now internally cited as a mandatory study example. Classification: MEDICAL EMERGENCY — Paranormal Negative. Note appended: 'This case could have resulted in fatalities had religious consultation continued to delay environmental investigation.'

name: Bhangarh Fort — Classified Observation
source: IPS Field Investigation — Partial Public Disclosure Only
description: Bhangarh Fort is the only location in India officially restricted by the Archaeological Survey of India after sunset. The ASI restriction is framed publicly as a safety measure due to structural instability. IPS conducted authorized daytime observation and a monitored after-hours investigation under special research clearance obtained in 2013. Gaurav Tiwari personally led the after-hours session. The public summary filed with ASI following the investigation concluded: no electromagnetic anomalies detected, structural instability confirmed in eastern sections, local acoustic phenomena (echo distortion) documented and attributed to the fort's architecture. What the public summary does not include: three pages of the original field log submitted internally to IPS are marked RESTRICTED and were separated from the public-facing report before filing. The contents of these pages have not been disclosed. Team members present during the investigation have not commented publicly on the discrepancy. Tiwari's personal notes from this investigation were not recovered after his death in 2016. Status: PARTIALLY DISCLOSED. Conclusion: WITHHELD.

name: Lambi Dehar Mines — Mass Death Site
source: IPS Investigation Records / Public Historical Documentation
description: The Lambi Dehar limestone mines operated near Mussoorie from the 1950s until closure in the early 1990s. Official records acknowledge the deaths of over 50,000 workers across the operational period, primarily from silicosis — a progressive and fatal lung disease caused by chronic inhalation of fine limestone dust. Workers were provided no protective equipment. No compensation was awarded. The mines were sealed following closure. Since then, the surrounding area has accumulated a documented pattern of reports: apparitions observed near the sealed mine entrance, unexplained sounds described as breathing or coughing from within sealed sections, multiple visitors reporting sudden onset of respiratory symptoms not attributable to physical causes. IPS investigators identified high residual limestone particulate still suspended in the air around the site — sufficient to cause mild respiratory symptoms in healthy adults during extended exposure. Psychological profiling of visitor experiences correlated strongly with prior knowledge of the site's history. Psychosomatic and environmental factors confirmed. However: the consistency of the breathing sounds reported by independent visitors who arrived without prior knowledge of the site's history has not been fully resolved in IPS documentation. That portion of the investigation is listed as: PENDING ADDITIONAL DATA.

name: Mukesh Mills — Observation Log
source: IPS Mumbai Investigation / Gaurav Tiwari Field Notes
description: Mukesh Mills was a major textile factory destroyed by fire in 1892. The ruins remain standing and have become a documented location for unexplained reports and a popular film production site. Multiple film crews across different decades have logged incidents: unexplained equipment failure concentrated in the same structural section, crew members independently reporting voices from areas confirmed to be empty, one documented case of an actress experiencing acute panic without stated cause during an overnight shoot. IPS conducted a multi-night observation with full equipment deployment. EMF readings showed no anomalies. Temperature readings were consistent with the open structure. Audio recording captured what investigators described as 'consistent low-frequency resonance patterns' — confirmed as infrasound generated by sea wind passing through the specific geometry of the ruined walls. Primary cause of reported phenomena: identified. Three observations from Night 2 of the investigation remain logged without conclusion in the field notes. The notes describe these observations in detail but offer no explanatory hypothesis. They are preserved exactly as written. IPS classification: PRIMARILY RESOLVED — Three Items Pending.

name: The Janakpuri Entity Report
source: Documented Witness Accounts / IPS Internal — Not Publicly Released
description: In the final week of June 2016, IPS founder Gaurav Tiwari and a small team investigated a case in Janakpuri, West Delhi involving a woman who reported being simultaneously haunted by multiple entities. The case was accepted as routine. The investigation took place over two nights. What happened during those two nights has not been formally documented in any publicly accessible IPS record. What is documented: accounts from two team members who stated that Tiwari appeared visibly disturbed by the end of the second night, and that he spoke to them briefly about feeling 'pulled' toward something he could not describe. These accounts were given to journalists after Tiwari's death on July 7, 2016 — seven days after the Janakpuri investigation concluded. Tiwari was found dead at his Dwarka residence. Delhi Police ruled suicide. His family disputed the ruling and stated he had shown no signs of depression. His father stated publicly that his son had mentioned feeling 'pulled by a negative force' approximately one month before his death. The Janakpuri case file exists in IPS internal records. It has not been released. The client has not been publicly identified. The full investigation log has not been recovered. Status: OPEN. Conclusion: NONE.

name: Dumas Beach — The Vanishing Reports
source: Gujarat Police Records / Local Documentation / Paranormal Research
description: Dumas Beach near Surat has accumulated a documented pattern of visitor disappearances since the 1990s. The beach is notable for its black sand — the result of centuries of ash from cremation grounds that historically occupied the site. Local residents do not swim at the beach after sunset. Gujarat Police records acknowledge multiple persons reported missing in the area, though official investigations have not established any common cause. The disappearances are not clustered by season, weather, or tidal patterns. Paranormal researchers visiting the site have documented consistent reports from locals of hearing voices calling from the water at night, particularly during new moon periods. No bodies have ever been recovered from the reported disappearance locations. An independent investigation in 2011 attempted to correlate the disappearances with rip current patterns — the analysis was inconclusive. The missing persons cases remain open. The beach is technically public and accessible at all hours. Why so many people have disappeared from a specific section of coastline over several decades has not been answered.

name: Shaniwar Wada — The Screaming Fort
description: Shaniwar Wada is a fortification built in 1732 in Pune, serving as the seat of the Peshwas of the Maratha Empire until 1818. In 1773, the fifth Peshwa Narayanrao was murdered within the fort's walls by guards acting on orders from his uncle and aunt. Witnesses documented that his final cry for help — directed at his uncle — echoed through the structure before he was killed. The fort partially burned in 1828 under unknown circumstances. Since then, and consistently documented through the 20th century, visitors and guards have reported hearing screaming at night, particularly on full moon evenings. Maharashtra state authorities have investigated the acoustic properties of the remaining structure. The fort's specific architectural dimensions create an unusual echo chamber effect — sounds from outside the fort can travel through the structure and emerge distorted. Whether the screaming sounds recorded by multiple independent witnesses have an acoustic explanation within this framework has not been conclusively demonstrated. The fort is a protected monument. Night access is restricted. Guards assigned to the site are rotated every three months.

name: D7 Sector — Bhuli Bhatiyari Ka Mahal
description: Bhuli Bhatiyari Ka Mahal is a 14th-century hunting lodge ruin located within what is now Karol Bagh, New Delhi. The structure's name translates loosely to 'the palace of the lost Bhatiyari woman' — a reference to a legend that the ruins are occupied by the spirit of a woman who died there, though no historical record confirms the story's origin. The ruins are technically accessible during daylight hours but attract minimal official attention. A pattern of reports from people living near the site and visiting urban explorers describes a persistent sensation of being observed, particularly in the eastern section of the ruins. Three separate groups have documented sudden battery drain in electronic equipment within a specific 20-meter radius of the eastern wall — a phenomenon that has not been reproduced under controlled conditions. Delhi Archaeological Survey has no active investigation open at the site. The ruins continue to degrade.

name: Dow Hill — The Headless Walker
source: West Bengal District Records / Multiple Investigation Reports
description: Dow Hill in Kurseong, West Bengal is associated with one of the most consistently reported paranormal accounts in northeastern India — the repeated, independent sighting of a headless figure walking in the surrounding forest. The accounts span decades and have been reported by local residents, school staff, forest workers, and visitors who had no prior knowledge of the legend before their encounter. The Victoria Boys High School nearby has its own documented history: a corridor leading to the woodlands is identified by former students and staff as a location of persistent cold spots, unexplained sounds, and at least two documented incidents of people experiencing acute panic without identifiable cause. The forest surrounding the area has been the site of multiple murders over the years, according to police records — though whether this is statistically anomalous for the region has not been formally assessed. Investigators who have spent extended time at the site describe it as having a consistent and oppressive atmospheric quality that does not correspond to weather or physical factors. A full investigation has never been formally completed at this location. The headless walker reports remain undebunked and unexplained.

name: Agrasen Ki Baoli — The Step Well Signal
description: Agrasen Ki Baoli is a 60-meter long, 15-meter wide historical step well located in central New Delhi. Built during the medieval period, it descends 103 steps to the water level. Despite being a protected monument and popular tourist site, it maintains a documented reputation for unusual experiences. Visitors consistently report that the step well becomes progressively quieter as one descends — an acoustic anomaly that has been noted but not formally studied. At the lowest level, multiple visitors across different years have documented the same experience: a sudden and extreme reluctance to continue descending, described not as fear but as a specific physical sensation of resistance. Two visitors have reported hearing what they described as a voice at the bottom level telling them to leave. No physical presence was present in either documented case. The well's water is dark and its depth at the lower levels remains unmeasured in any accessible public record. Whether anyone has mapped what is at the bottom of Agrasen Ki Baoli is not documented.
